entry.title: Architecting Electronic Bill of Lading on SAP BTP - Blueprint (Part 2 of 3)
entry.content:
Introduction

In Part 1 , we explored the transformative potential of the Electronic Bill of Lading in international trade, highlighting its impact on sustainability, enhanced supply chain transparency, and accelerated business processes.

Part 2 presents the building blocks used to implement the solution and includes an architecture diagram illustrating how the SAP BTP services and components are integrated.

 

1. System Context

The ocean carrier already operates an established core system that manages transactional data such as bookings, shipping instructions, and transport events on SAP Cloud ERP( S/4HANA Transportation Management).

SAP Cloud ERP

 

2. SAP Business Technology Platform

The foundation of this solution is SAP BTP, a cloud platform that brings together application development, data management, integration, and AI capabilities. It provides the core infrastructure and services needed to build enterprise applications.

To support the transition to digital trade, the Electronic Bill of Lading capability is implemented on SAP BTP as a side-by-side extension.

 

3. Electronic Bill of Lading: TradeTrust Framework

The open-source TradeTrust Framework  serve as the foundation for implementing a fully digital and verifiable Electronic Bill of Lading solution. It can be deployed as a Node.js application on Cloud Foundry, which provides the runtime for developing and running cloud-native applications on SAP BTP.

On this PoC, the TradeTrust framework uses the Ethereum blockchain whereby the ERC-721 provides a widely-used smart contract API used on non-fungible tokens (NFTs) to allow transfer of ownership whilst providing assurance of integrity, singularity, and control.

Blockchain technology is a decentralized digital ledger system that records and verifies transactions across a network of computers in a secure and transparent manner. It consists of a chain of blocks, each containing a list of transactions thatare linked and secured using cryptographic techniques. Once a block is added to the chain, its data become immutable through a distributed ledger system, ensuring the integrity and permanence of the transaction records. This immutability and transparency enable blockchain to facilitate the exchange of anything of value, whether tangible assets including physical goods or intangible items such as digital assets.[11]

 

4. Terminal Operating System

Terminal Operating System (TOS) is the port terminal’s operational platform that manage and monitor container and vessel activities. Leveraging IoT-enabled equipment and sensors, the TOS generates real-time operational events such as container loading, vessel arrival, and departure. These events are distributed through a publish–subscribe model, pushing notifications to a configured endpoint. To ensure authenticity and integrity, the messages are encrypted and digitally signed.

5. Event-Driven Architecture

SAP Integration Suite running on SAP BTP provides the capabilities that enable Event-Driven Architecture within the solution. The Cloud Integration is the middleware layer responsible for routing, transforming, and exchanging messages between external systems and SAP services. Under the hood, it uses the open-source Apache Camel Framework, enabling integration flows based on enterprise integration patterns.

For event orchestration SAP Event Mesh is the message broker, it facilitates asynchronous communication by receiving events from the terminal/port and routing them to the subscribed consumer Events-To-Business Actions Framework, that leverages the decisions capability of SAP Build Process Automation to initiate business actions.

 

6. Automation

For the process automation, a workflow is managed on SAP Build Process Automation 

 

7. UI

Stakeholders access the application through SAP Build Work Zone, where authentication is handled by SAP Cloud Identity Services integrated with a custom Identity Provider (IdP) that validates blockchain wallet signatures. Authorization is then enforced via role collections, ensuring access only to permitted functionalities.

 

8. Diagram

  1. The Terminal Operating System generates an operational event(Load, Departure, Arrival). The event is published to the event broker SAP Event Mesh.
  2. Event-to-Business-Actions framework is subscribed to SAP Event Mesh topics to receive the events.
  3. Based on the rule outcome, the processor triggers a workflow on SAP Build Process Automation to orchestrate the business process.
  4. The workflow calls SAP Integration Suite to start the process.
  5. An Integration Flow retrieves the required Ocean Freight Booking from SAP S/4HANA and builds the payload to issue the eBL.
  6. The Integration Flow invokes the TradeTrust Framework.
  7. The Electronic Bill of Lading is issued on Ethereum Network.

 

9. Data Context

 

Conclusion:

Part 2 outlined the essential building blocks and architectural components for implementing the eBL solution on SAP Business Technology Platform.

In Part 3, I’ll walk through a complete end-to-end global trade scenario, illustrating how the architecture operates in practice, from container loading event to eBL issuance, ownership transfer, and final surrender.

entry.title: Re: SAP datasphere task chain: sql procedure not visible
entry.content:
There are some points can be check.

1. Re-check the privilege assignment

2. Create a minimal test procedure to narrow down the scope

To determine whether this is a privilege issue or something specific to the procedure definition, can have a quick test:

  • Create a very simple test procedure in the same Open SQL schema (for example, without referencing other tables or objects).
  • Check whether this test procedure becomes visible during Task Chain modeling.

If the test procedure is visible, this usually indicates that the space-to-schema access and EXECUTE privilege are generally working, and the issue may be related to the original procedure’s definition or its dependent objects.

If the test procedure is not visible, this strongly points to a remaining authorization or execution-identity issue.

entry.title: Re: The BDLS table process was automatically for table /B...
entry.content:
1. Archive data as possible as you can.

Your current table size is too huge and too many records involved in table, 
these records is reaching or exceeding HANA table limit, this is your main problem.

Archive more data that should be the best approach for this, and this basically not a DB topic and need to be handled by your BW admin.

Your BW admin could refer to this note for more relevant.
2388483 - How-To: Data Management for Technical Tables

e.g. for the table /BIC/AZFC_D04100, 
go to refer to this topic - /<bw_prefix>/A*00,
then go to check this note:
3232938 - [BW Central KBA] How-To: Data Management for BW Infoprovider tables

2. Perform the Table Distribution as my coleague suggested.

The Table Distribution may bring best practise for table structure and must to be applied for BW system.

I checked the screen shot you attached in this case, it looks normal and I did not see any unexpected point there.

One thing you need to note, the execution of Table Distribution may bring high system load, you may need schedule it outside of business hour.

- reference
1908075 - SAP BW on SAP HANA: Table Placement and Landscape Redistribution
2334091 - SAP BW/4HANA: Table Placement and Landscape Redistribution

entry.title: Re: Valuation of produced material - moving average price
entry.content:
Good day @Anja_1 

Thank you for your question.

The answer to your question on the availability of a customisation to make the order output use total planned cost instead of current MAP is NO, not in the Public Cloud design for a V (average moving) price material. The output recipient is preliminarily valuated, and the system handles order differences later through settlements or event-based production accounting.

The Public Cloud provides two options:

  • Option 1 is for you to change the material to standard price control. This best supports in house manufactured materials. That also ensures your preliminary goods receipt closely follows the standard costing process.
  • Option 2 is to keep MAP but use Actual Costing. If your business needs valuation which is close to your actual production cost, then the recommended approach is to use 33Q Actual Costing. Here, the system handles actual price valuation in the actual costing run, not by changing the preliminary goods receipt valuation logic of the production order itself.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/4032610758dc437089f0c28320eec93f/01500bf05468400b873c792b38c44161.html?locale=en-US

3599914 - About the transition from the scope item BEI to the scope item 3F0 - SAP for Me

3445478 - Unable to find actual costing related APPs - - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

A constraint you are facing in your specific design is that the same material number is both consumed in the order and received again as output. This makes the price behaviour even more sensitive for a MAP material, because the system keeps using the moving average logic for the same stock identity. So, that result you see is consistent with standard MAP logic rather than the logic that drives a routing based finished good valuation.

So, if your business process is really an adjustment or refurbishment of the same material, then consider whether a rework order process is a better fit than producing the same MAP material into itself.

See the following references for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/7f47a6d9441c46ac86c96fd27f6015f0/bbd9b6ce0be14516ad7868da0982024a.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/4032610758dc437089f0c28320eec93f/9d75eaf09b2d4d3fae127b4e9f1fc69a.html?locale=en-US

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Can MRP prioritize one BOM alternative component and...
entry.content:
Hello @FaisalMaayeh 

Thank you for your question.

Question 1: In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, can MRP itself recommend the alternative component automatically based on stock availability?

Answer: Not in that way you described it. The reason is, MRP does not behave like a stock aware decision engine that first consumes B and only if B is short automatically replans with C as a fallback. MRP uses the usage probability to determine dependent requirements. See KBA 3316105 for more insights.

So, if your requirement is specifically for MRP to check actual stock of B first, and only if B is unavailable, automatically propose C instead, then the answer is no, not as standard MRP fallback option in Public Cloud.

See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/86fab5c5a10440f0a9dccdc6d761b196.html?locale=en-US

Question 2: Or is this only handled later by the planner in the production order?

Answer: The automatic stock-based fallback is not a standard behaviour. In practice, this requirement is typically resolved after planning through planner review and operation decision making rather than by expecting MRP itself to automatically make the substitution.

Question 3: If standard MRP does not support this exact fallback behavior, what is the best-practice approach in Public Cloud?

Answer: The best practice in Public Cloud is usually one of these:

  • Option A is for you to maintain alternative items in the BOM and let the planner decided the order
  • Option B is to use product substitution where the business scenario is product substitution, not BOM component substitution.
  • Option C is for you to review whether you need different planning design. If your business requirement is very strict and must be automatic, stock sensitive, and rules based during planning, then standard alternative item BOM may not be sufficient. In that case, you will need to redesign around the planning model rather than expecting MRP to provide a custom fallback algorithm

See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/8a7dea8cc1a4421e97acb2463f544b2b.html?locale=en-US

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Introducing the First Demand Handover Process in SAP IBP Harmonized Planning Area
entry.content:
We are pleased to share that the first scenario for handing over demand between time‑series planning and order-based planning is now available in the SAP IBP Harmonized Planning Area (HPA). This process helps organizations create a more seamless flow of information from strategic and tactical planning into operational execution.

The new process uses forecast consumption based on sales order data coming through Real-Time Integration (RTI). After consumption, the remaining open forecast is transferred from weekly planning to the daily level so it can be used directly by order-based planning.

To enable real-time forecast consumption, the process relies on key figure calculations, ensuring that customers have a consistent and transparent way to prepare demand before it hands over into order-based planning.

With this first scenario in place, customers can run demand, S&OP, and operational planning on harmonized data while maintaining clear ownership between the planning layers. It reduces manual steps and helps planners ensure that the demand used in supply and order planning remains aligned with the latest business inputs.

Concept for Demand Handover on PRODLOC level

This section outlines key design considerations and best practices for configuring and utilizing forecast consumption within SAP Integrated Business Planning (IBP) at the Product-Location (PRODLOC) planning level.
The following points detail various aspects of this process, including system behavior, recommended configurations, and specific scenarios.

  • Time-series planning is performed on the level of WKPRODLOCTSPCUST (week, product, location, time-series planning customer)
  • Forecast consumption is configured on the level of WKPRODLOC using the sales order information integrated via Real-Time Integration (RTI)
    • If forecast consumption is configured using the simplified key figure calculation, we recommend keeping the calculation graph small. This approach helps mitigating potential performance impacts, as complex graphs can lead to time-consuming operations. Furthermore, note that restrictions apply to the output key figures for the IBP_CONSUMPTION function (see SAP help)
  • For periods shorter than six weeks the forecast from demand sensing can be used to leverage its short-term accuracy. For periods beyond six weeks the forecast from IBP demand can be used.

We will describe here a case where it is not possible to assign a sales order with an Order-Based Planning (OBP) customer to a TSP customer. Then the aggregation is only possible on WKPRODLOC/DAYPRODLOC level.

  • If demand sensing is not in use, the recommendation is to disaggregate the open forecast on daily level using the number of working days per calendar week.
  • The open forecast, resulting from the forecast consumption, is aggregated on the level of DAYPRODLOC (i.e. without any TSP or OBP customer information)
  • As the automated forecast consumption from SAP7F is not available in HPA we recommend using the keyfigure calculation or forecast consumption job.
  • The remaining open forecast can either be directly used as input to OBP via a calculated key figure or through a release process using a copy operator. Note that restrictions apply to the output key figures of the IBP_CONSUMPTION function.

entry.title: Re: CBC Scope Item Deletion: Does it remain active in the...
entry.content:
Good day @UmenoYagi 

Thank you for your question.

Question: If a scope item that was previously activated in CBC is deleted by SAP, does it remain active and functional in the customer system?

Answer: Yes for depreciated scope item, when you have already activated it in your system. It does not automatically disappear from your tenant. The only thing you should have in mind is, due to the depreciation, the functionality will remain static. In other words, it will not be updated by SAP, and some functions may still remain usable.

Regarding which changes occur, the main change is usually:

  • The scope item is no longer available for new activation or new scoping
  • Your current related configuration is kept
  • As mentioned above SAP will no longer update that scope item.
  • Ensure you monitor the release information and the availability for successor or impacts you may experience due to the removal.

Here is a clarification you need to be aware of.

There is a difference between:

  1. Deprecated/no longer offered for scoping. In this situation, active customer configuration is not deleted and not updated.
  2. A feature or app being fully removed in a future release. In this scenario, make sure you consult the SAP What’s New documentation.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: What is the state of DOE?
entry.content: DOE in the SAP SUP context is kind of aging tech now, so a lot of setups depend heavily on the legacy landscape you’re already running. Most modern mobility strategies tend to shift toward Gateway + newer architectures, but DOE still shows up in older offline-first deployments. I’ve seen a similar practical overview on Excedify that explains where DOE fits in real systems: www.excedify.com/bundles/gdt-certification-pathway

entry.title: Limiting Output Tables and Columns in SWNC_GET_WORKLOAD_SNAPSHOT via JCo
entry.content:
Hi all,

We are using SAP JCo to call the RFC function module SWNC_GET_WORKLOAD_SNAPSHOT, which returns multiple output tables such as MEMORY, WEBC, WEBSD, DBCON etc.

We have a couple of questions regarding optimizing the data retrieval:

  1. Is there any way to limit the number of output tables returned by this function module?
    For example, can we restrict the response to only specific tables like MEMORY?
  2. When retrieving a specific table (e.g., MEMORY), is it possible to limit the number of columns returned?
    For instance, we may only need 4–5 fields instead of the entire structure, mainly to improve performance and reduce data transfer.

    Any suggestions or best practices would be helpful.
    Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Is it mandatory to tag pay component in Pension fund even if storing for info purpose only
entry.content:
HI All , 

Our client is using Global Benefits to store and reflect info at single platform. For Pension - admin are uploading the file with value that employee has opted in start of month and captured in separate tool. 

Client does not want any pay component to created or any data to be propagated for Pension to comp info hence , we have keep it as Benefit integration and over to this , as client also doesnt want to create irrelevant pay components as part of foundation data . 

Hence , we have created the pension fund without pay component which system currently allows as well. 

But we wanted to ensure - if this is against good practice or if there are any other impacts with such config. 

As of now , we do not see any challenges as this suffices the client requirement. 

 

SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Global Benefits

entry.title: Re: SAP datasphere task chain: sql procedure not visible
entry.content:
Hello Severilar,

If you want to add some procedure in Task Chain,  Please ensure that there are no parameters in the procedure or that the following parameter types are used：

  • "NCHAR", "STRING", "NVARCHAR", "ALPHANUM", "SHORTTEXT", "INT", "TINYINT", "BIGINT", "CHAR", "DECIMAL", "SMALLDECIMAL", "INTEGER", "SMALLINT", "REAL", "DOUBLE", "FLOAT", "VARCHAR", "BOOLEAN", "DATE", "TIME", "SECONDDATE", "TIMESTAMP"

entry.title: Re: SAP for me error message when displaying installation...
entry.content:
Hi customer,

In order to check the installation data in SAP for Me, the S-user needs 'Manage Installations' authorization assigned either on CCC level, customer number level or installation number level.

Please refer to KBA 3469559 - Authorization required to view my company's installation numbers - SAP for Me. 

Please kindly check and make sure your S-user has this authorization properly assigned.

If not, then please request for the authorization using the self-service feature as introduced in KBA 2891528 - How to use the Self-Service Authorization Request - SAP for Me.

Hope this helps.

Best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Making Supplier Compliance Easier with SAP Ariba Supplier Management
entry.content:
In this blog post, you will learn about how supplier compliance becomes more manageable when expectations are clear, supplier data stays current, and risk signals are monitored throughout the lifecycle.  

Supplier compliance is easiest when expectations are clear, supplier data stays current, and risks are monitored throughout the lifecycle—not just at onboarding. Many issues arise later, when profiles become outdated, or documents expire, slowing sourcing, and increasing exposure. 

A structured approach helps prevent this by setting clear requirements, standardizing onboarding, enabling simple self‑service updates, and routing reviews based on risk. This reduces follow‑ups and closes gaps early. 

Continuous monitoring adds further stability with alerts for expiring documents, visibility into changing risk indicators, updated performance data, and fewer surprises during contracting. 

Strong governance—defined approvals, clear data ownership, routine reviews, and escalation paths—keeps internal teams aligned. 

When registration, qualification, contracting, and monitoring operate as one connected process, suppliers face fewer repeated requests and can maintain accurate profiles, resulting in smoother collaboration overall. 

You can find the full document on the Spend Management Value Map, which covers practical steps, design guidance, and SAP Ariba Supplier Management capabilities that strengthen compliance across the supplier lifecycle. It highlights how steady improvements reduce complexity, maintain readiness, and support healthier supplier relationships. 

Enhance Your SAP Experience with Value Maps  

Beyond improving supplier compliance, SAP Enterprise Support value maps on SAP Community provides guidance, recommended actions, and supporting resources to help you decide what to focus on next—all included in your SAP cloud subscription.   

👉 Watch short demo video, then head over to our page - explore our range of topics and request a call with a topic expert for guidance tailored to your goals.   

About the author:  

Rachana is a Topic Expert for SAP Enterprise Support value maps, specializing in Spend Management. With over 15 years at SAP, she brings deep experience from a range of customer-facing roles. She enjoys working closely with customers to help them make the most of SAP Enterprise Support and drive real results.  

💡 Have questions or feedback around this content? Don’t hesitate to comment down below or contact @Rachana_Rasania

entry.title: Notification and SleepMode Jobs in IBP
entry.content:
Hello all,

I have a job that consists of multiple steps.

My goal is to add two additional steps at the beginning:

  1. A job that notifies selected users that another job will start shortly. (I could not find a suitable job template for this—are there any recommended solutions?)
  2. A job (or alternative approach) that introduces a 5-minute delay before the next step begins. (I was unable to find a job template for this as well.)

The purpose of these two initial steps is to notify users and provide them with a 5-minute buffer before Step 3 starts.

Does anyone have suggestions on how this could be implemented?

Thank you.

Best regards,
Ram

entry.title: Re: cXML for Service type requisitions lines
entry.content:
Good day @Stefanoli4 

Thank you for your question.

In Public Cloud, punchout is an OCI scenario, not as a cXML-based service item typing scenario. See the following reference for more details.

3551234 - Maintaining HOOK_URL value for Punchout scenario - SAP for Me

2860449 - Implementation of the BADI MMPUR_OCI_ITEM_TRANSFER_DATA - SAP for Me

The behaviour is that catalogs return product items, while service items are created in the purchase requisition process itself. If you need to enhance returned punchout data, you can use the extension point BAdI MMPUR_OCI_ITEM_TRANSFER_DATA.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/713eae2544204816b71a88caa89bd256.html?locale=en-US

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Can MRP prioritize one BOM alternative component and...
entry.content:
Hi Chris,

Thank you for the detailed explanation and the KBAs. I think I see where the confusion might be between the "Planning" and "Execution" stages.

I have been testing this in our S/4HANA Public Cloud environment, and the system is actually performing the fallback, but perhaps not during the MRP run itself. Here is what I observed (see attached screenshots):

  1. The Setup: I maintained an Alternative Item Group in the BOM for Product A. I set Material B to Priority 1 (100% usage) and Material C to Priority 2 (0% usage) with Strategy 2 (100% check).

  2. The Result: * Pic 1: When Material B has stock, the Process Order correctly selects B.

    • Pic 2: When Material B is out of stock, the system automatically allocates the requirement to Material C during the Availability Check (ATP) in the Process Order.

My Question to clarify the misunderstanding: Is it correct to say that while MRP (Procurement/Planning) will always stay "blind" and keep requesting Material B, the Process Order (Execution) is indeed "stock-aware" and handles the fallback to C automatically via the Alternative Item Group logic?

If this is the case, the system technically meets our requirement for production to keep moving, even if the "shopping list" from MRP stays focused on the primary material. Does this align with your "Option A," or is there a specific risk in the Public Cloud we should be aware of by relying on the ATP check to do this swap?

entry.title: Calling the SAP Business Partner API from a Kyma Serverless Function via BTP Destination Service
entry.content:
Introduction

One of the most common integration patterns in SAP BTP is connecting a Kyma serverless function to an external SAP API — securely, without hardcoding credentials. In this post I'll walk through exactly how to do that using the BTP Destination Service, a Kyma ServiceBinding, and the SAP API Business Accelerator Hub sandbox.

By the end you'll have a working Node.js Kyma function that:

  1. Obtains an OAuth token from XSUAA using client credentials
  2. Resolves a named BTP Destination to get the target API URL
  3. Calls the SAP Business Partner API (A_BusinessPartner) and returns JSON results

All credentials are stored in Kubernetes secrets — nothing is hardcoded.

Prerequisites

  • SAP BTP Trial account with a Kyma cluster enabled
  • kubectl configured against your Kyma cluster
  • An account on api.sap.com with an API key for the sandbox
  • VSCode with the Kubernetes extension (optional but useful)

Step 1: Enable the Serverless Module in Kyma

By default, the Serverless module may not be enabled on your Kyma cluster. Check:

kubectl get crd | grep serverless

If nothing is returned, go to your BTP cockpit, open the Kyma cluster overview, click Modules → Add, and enable Serverless. Wait a few minutes and recheck.

Step 2: Create the BTP Destination Service Instance

We need a Destination Service instance to store and resolve our API destination. Rather than creating it through the BTP cockpit UI (which has limitations on Trial for Kyma runtime), we use the BTP Service Operator directly in Kubernetes.

First verify the BTP Service Operator CRDs are available:

kubectl get crd | grep sap

You should see serviceinstances.services.cloud.sap.com and servicebindings.services.cloud.sap.com.

Create k8s/destination-instance.yaml:

apiVersion: services.cloud.sap.com/v1
kind: ServiceInstance
metadata:
  name: destination-service
  namespace: default
spec:
  serviceOfferingName: destination
  servicePlanName: lite

Apply it:

kubectl apply -f k8s/destination-instance.yaml
kubectl get serviceinstances -n default

Wait until the status shows Ready.

Step 3: Create the Service Binding

The binding injects the Destination Service credentials (XSUAA client ID, client secret, token URL, and service URI) into a Kubernetes secret.

Create k8s/destination-binding.yaml:

apiVersion: services.cloud.sap.com/v1
kind: ServiceBinding
metadata:
  name: destination-service-binding
  namespace: default
spec:
  serviceInstanceName: destination-service

Apply it:

kubectl apply -f k8s/destination-binding.yaml
kubectl get servicebindings -n default

Verify the secret was created and check the keys:

kubectl get secret destination-service-binding -n default -o jsonpath='{.data}' | python3 -m json.tool

You'll see keys including clientid, clientsecret, url (XSUAA token endpoint), and uri (Destination Service endpoint).

Step 4: Create the BTP Destination

In BTP cockpit go to Connectivity → Destinations → New Destination → From Scratch and fill in:

Field Value

NameS4H_SANDBOX_BP
TypeHTTP
URLhttps://sandbox.api.sap.com/s4hanacloud/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER
AuthenticationNoAuthentication
Additional Property: APIKeyyour api.sap.com API key

Save it. The API key is stored as a destination property — the function will pass it as a request header.

Step 5: Store the API Key as a Kubernetes Secret

Never put API keys in your function code or YAML. Create a secret:

kubectl create secret generic bp-function-apikey \
  --from-literal=apikey='<your-api-key>' \
  -n default

Step 6: Enable Istio Sidecar Injection

The Kyma APIRule requires Istio to be injected into the function pod. Label the namespace:

kubectl label namespace default istio-injection=enabled --overwrite

Step 7: Deploy the Function

Here is the complete k8s/function.yaml. The inline source contains the full Node.js function — no external dependencies required beyond Node built-ins.

apiVersion: serverless.kyma-project.io/v1alpha2
kind: Function
metadata:
  name: bp-function
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: bp-function
spec:
  runtime: nodejs20
  source:
    inline:
      dependencies: |
        {
          "name": "kyma-bp-function",
          "version": "1.0.0",
          "description": "Kyma serverless function to fetch SAP Business Partners via BTP Destination Service",
          "main": "handler.js",
          "engines": {
            "node": ">=18"
          }
        }
      source: |
        const https = require("https");
        const http = require("http");
        const zlib = require("zlib");

        async function getDestinationToken() {
          const credentials = Buffer.from(
            `${process.env.CLIENTID}:${process.env.CLIENTSECRET}`
          ).toString("base64");
          const tokenUrl = new URL(`${process.env.XSUAA_URL}/oauth/token`);
          const body = "grant_type=client_credentials";

          return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
            const options = {
              hostname: tokenUrl.hostname,
              path: `${tokenUrl.pathname}?${body}`,
              method: "POST",
              headers: {
                Authorization: `Basic ${credentials}`,
                "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded",
              },
            };
            const req = https.request(options, (res) => {
              let data = "";
              res.on("data", (chunk) => (data += chunk));
              res.on("end", () => {
                try {
                  resolve(JSON.parse(data).access_token);
                } catch (e) {
                  reject(new Error(`Failed to parse token response: ${data}`));
                }
              });
            });
            req.on("error", reject);
            req.end();
          });
        }

        async function getDestination(destinationName, token) {
          const destUrl = new URL(
            `${process.env.DESTINATION_URI}/destination-configuration/v1/destinations/${destinationName}`
          );
          return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
            const options = {
              hostname: destUrl.hostname,
              path: destUrl.pathname,
              method: "GET",
              headers: {
                Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`,
              },
            };
            const req = https.request(options, (res) => {
              let data = "";
              res.on("data", (chunk) => (data += chunk));
              res.on("end", () => {
                try {
                  resolve(JSON.parse(data));
                } catch (e) {
                  reject(new Error(`Failed to parse destination response: ${data}`));
                }
              });
            });
            req.on("error", reject);
            req.end();
          });
        }

        async function fetchBusinessPartners(destination) {
          const destUrl = destination.destinationConfiguration;
          const targetUrl = new URL(`${destUrl.URL}/A_BusinessPartner?$format=json&$top=20`);
          const protocol = targetUrl.protocol === "https:" ? https : http;
          return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
            const options = {
              hostname: targetUrl.hostname,
              path: `${targetUrl.pathname}${targetUrl.search}`,
              method: "GET",
              headers: {
                APIKey: process.env.API_KEY,
                Accept: "application/json",
              },
            };
            const req = protocol.request(options, (res) => {
              const chunks = [];
              const encoding = res.headers["content-encoding"];
              const stream = encoding === "gzip" ? res.pipe(zlib.createGunzip())
                           : encoding === "deflate" ? res.pipe(zlib.createInflate())
                           : res;
              stream.on("data", (chunk) => chunks.push(chunk));
              stream.on("end", () => {
                const raw = Buffer.concat(chunks).toString("utf8");
                try {
                  resolve({ statusCode: res.statusCode, body: JSON.parse(raw) });
                } catch (e) {
                  resolve({ statusCode: res.statusCode, body: raw });
                }
              });
              stream.on("error", reject);
            });
            req.on("error", reject);
            req.end();
          });
        }

        module.exports = {
          main: async function (event, context) {
            const DESTINATION_NAME = "S4H_SANDBOX_BP";
            try {
              const token = await getDestinationToken();
              const destination = await getDestination(DESTINATION_NAME, token);
              const result = await fetchBusinessPartners(destination);
              return {
                statusCode: result.statusCode,
                headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
                body: result.body,
              };
            } catch (err) {
              console.error("Error:", err.message);
              return {
                statusCode: 500,
                headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
                body: { error: err.message },
              };
            }
          },
        };
  resourceConfiguration:
    function:
      resources:
        requests:
          cpu: 50m
          memory: 64Mi
        limits:
          cpu: 100m
          memory: 128Mi
  env:
    - name: CLIENTID
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: destination-service-binding
          key: clientid
    - name: CLIENTSECRET
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: destination-service-binding
          key: clientsecret
    - name: XSUAA_URL
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: destination-service-binding
          key: url
    - name: DESTINATION_URI
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: destination-service-binding
          key: uri
    - name: API_KEY
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: bp-function-apikey
          key: apikey

Apply it:

kubectl apply -f k8s/function.yaml
kubectl get functions -n default

Wait for RUNNING: True.

Step 8: Expose the Function with an APIRule

Create k8s/apirule.yaml using the v2 API (v1beta1 is deprecated):

apiVersion: gateway.kyma-project.io/v2
kind: APIRule
metadata:
  name: bp-function
  namespace: default
spec:
  gateway: kyma-system/kyma-gateway
  hosts:
    - bp-function.<your-cluster-domain>.kyma.ondemand.com
  rules:
    - methods:
        - GET
      noAuth: true
      path: /*
  service:
    name: bp-function
    port: 80

Get your cluster domain:

kubectl get gateway -n kyma-system kyma-gateway -o jsonpath='{.spec.servers[0].hosts[0]}'

Apply and check status:

kubectl apply -f k8s/apirule.yaml
kubectl get apirule bp-function -n default -o jsonpath='{.status.state}'

Step 9: Test It

curl https://bp-function.<your-cluster-domain>.kyma.ondemand.com

You should get a JSON response containing Business Partner records from the SAP sandbox:

{
  "statusCode": 200,
  "body": {
    "d": {
      "results": [
        {
          "BusinessPartner": "1000037",
          "BusinessPartnerFullName": "...",
          ...
        }
      ]
    }
  }
}

How It Works

The flow is:

  1. The function starts and reads XSUAA credentials from the Kubernetes secret injected by the BTP Service Operator
  2. It calls the XSUAA token endpoint with client credentials to get a bearer token
  3. It uses that token to call the BTP Destination Service and resolve the S4H_SANDBOX_BP destination — getting back the target URL and any stored properties
  4. It calls the SAP API sandbox using the resolved URL, passing the API key as a header
  5. The response is gzip-decoded (the sandbox always returns gzip) and returned as JSON

A Note on Gzip

The SAP API Hub sandbox always returns gzip-compressed responses. Node's https module does not automatically decompress these. The function handles this explicitly using Node's built-in zlib module, detecting the content-encoding response header and piping through zlib.createGunzip() or zlib.createInflate() as appropriate.

Key Points

No external npm packages — the function uses only Node.js built-ins (https, http, zlib), keeping the deployment simple and fast.

Credentials never touch code or git — XSUAA credentials come from the BTP Service Operator binding secret, the API key from a manually created Kubernetes secret. Neither appears in any YAML committed to source control.

Destination Service as the integration layer — by resolving the destination at runtime rather than hardcoding the URL, you can swap the backend (sandbox vs. production, different regions) just by changing the BTP destination configuration, with no code changes.

Istio sidecar is required — the Kyma APIRule validation requires the function pod to have the Istio sidecar injected. Make sure you label the namespace with istio-injection=enabled before deploying the function.

What's Next

From here you could extend this pattern to:

  • Query specific Business Partners by key
  • POST data back to the API
  • Chain multiple API calls within a single function
  • Trigger the function from a Kyma event rather than an HTTP call
  • Deploy to a production BTP account pointing at a real S/4HANA system rather than the sandbox

The same pattern — BTP Destination Service + Kyma ServiceBinding + serverless function — works for any SAP API on the Business Accelerator Hub, and for non-SAP APIs too.

entry.title: Q1 2026 Key Technical Updates for SAP Commerce Cloud
entry.content:
The SAP Commerce Cloud 2211 continuous innovation release model offers substantial benefits by ensuring faster access to the latest features, enhancements, and security updates. This approach allows for seamless and frequent updates, minimizing disruptions while maintaining a robust and high-performing e-commerce platform. You can take advantage of new innovations quickly, stay ahead of market demands, and maintain competitive agility, all while reducing the risk associated with traditional major version cycles.
 
To successfully operate and plan with SAP Commerce Cloud, we strongly recommend focusing on the following key areas: 
  1. Stay current with SAP Commerce Cloud monthly and Framework Update Releases
  2. Stay current on Composable Storefront and compatible with its dependencies (Node.js, Angular)
  3. Review validity and compatibility of all features, services, and updates (e.g. Solr, Extensions)

 

1 – Stay Current with SAP Commerce Cloud Monthly and Framework Update Releases

 
Staying up to date with the latest SAP Commerce Cloud and Composable Storefront releases ensures access to the latest enhancements, bug fixes, performance improvements, and security updates. 
 
To assist you in preparing for upcoming changes and implementations, SAP publishes the Notification of Key Technical Updates on the SAP Help Portal. This continuously updated documentation highlights when changes are available, including features and services that have an adoption window. We strongly recommend bookmarking this page and reviewing it regularly to remain informed of relevant technical updates. 
 
As part of the platform’s continuous evolution, SAP Commerce Cloud now aligns with Java 21 and Spring 6, establishing a modern, secure, and high‑performance technology foundation. To remain on a supported update and avoid build failures, SAP advises to adopt the Framework Update no later than June 30, 2026, when the final Java 17 release reaches end of support coverage. At the very latest, please ensure the update is completed by August 31, 2026, after which new builds targeting Java 17 will be disallowed. 
 
Please note that the update removes deprecated extensions and modules and could necessitate code refactoring or configuration updates.
 
This quarter, the Framework Update should have the highest priority in your planning strategy. Carefully read the information below:
  • Framework Update Overview (JDK 21, Spring 6)
  • Dependencies of Framework Update:
    • OAuth replacement - The existing OAuth capability has been rebuilt on current Spring libraries.
    • Drools Update - The previous Drools version 8.44.0. Final is incompatible with Spring 6, as the Java EE API has been upgraded to the Jakarta EE API in Spring 6. 
    • 3rd Party Libraries - Custom implementations may include 3rd party libraries that are not part of the SAP Commerce Cloud out-of-the-box product. These dependencies must likewise be identified, reviewed, and validated to ensure compatibility.
    • Integrations - An up-to-date inventory of all Independent Software Vendor (ISV) and partner integrations connected to or complementing SAP Commerce Cloud implementations should be maintained, such as payment providers, tax solutions, and other direct integrations, with proactive coordination across implementation partners and software vendors to verify ongoing compatibility.
  • Deprecation of Extensions with the Framework Update:
    • SAP has removed several extensions from Commerce Cloud and from the Integration Extension Pack due to incompatibilities with the newest versions of Java and the Spring framework. Replacement solutions already exist for each use case and are documented on the SAP Help Portal. These deprecated extensions are highlighted on the Notification of Key Technical Updates SAP Help page. Use the "Production Version" filter for September 2025 for a complete overview review the following:

      Deletion of Deprecated Cockpit Extensions
      Deletion of Deprecated amazoncloud Extension
      Deletion of Deprecated Sales Order Simulation Extensions
      Deletion of Deprecated Customer Order Sourcing Extensions
      Deletion of Synchronous Order Management as well as the SAP For Me Note
 
The removal of the CMS Cockpit and migration paths to SmartEdit are further documented here:
  • Migrating from CMS Cockpit to SmartEdit_Solution Overview.mp4  
  • Getting Started with SmartEdit 
  • SmartEdit Feature Parity
  • Technical Overview of SmartEdit
For more information on the Framework Update, refer to the following documentation:
  • Framework Update – Detailed Overview
  • SAP Commerce Cloud Framework Update FAQ
  • SAP Help Framework Update 
  • SAP Commerce Cloud Release Ready Kit
  • List of supported SAP Commerce Cloud releases
For instructions on enabling new features and functionality, see: 
  • Enabling SAP Commerce Cloud 
  • Feature Flags in the Composable Storefront documentation

 

2 – Stay Current on Composable Storefront and Compatible with its Dependencies

Composable Storefront

 
SAP Commerce Cloud ensures that all new releases of Composable Storefront are fully tested and compatible with the current 2211 Commerce Cloud releases. Please note that older backend updates are not supported. To avoid issues, review the list of supported Composable Storefront releases below and confirm you are running an update that SAP supports.
 
To adopt the upcoming Framework Update, you will need to upgrade to the latest Composable Storefront release. For added flexibility, SAP Commerce Cloud will continue to support Composable Storefront on the current framework (JDK 17) until August 31, 2026. After this date:
  • Any Composable Storefront version earlier than a valid 221121.x update, will no longer be eligible to go live on the Framework Update.
  • New builds will be blocked for unsupported versions and update releases.
Also, in the March 2026 update of Composable Storefront, the new authorizationCodeFlowByDefault authentication flow will be enabled by default. See More
 
To maintain build and deployment capability, please upgrade to a current update of 221121.x before August 31, 2026. See More

Node.js 20 

Additionally, building Composable Storefront and SSR hosting is only supported with the latest supported versions of Node.js, provided by the Cloud Portal. Note that support ended on January 1, 2026 for Node.js 20. Please review the dependencies matrix below and migrate to Node.js 22 as soon as possible, if you have not yet done so, to ensure compatibility and continued support.

Angular 

As part of our yearly update cycle, Angular has been upgraded from Angular 19 to 21 on February 20, 2026. This framework update enables standalone components, while keeping Modules for backwards compatibility. Plan ahead to align your projects with this update. See More
 
For more information, refer to the following documentation:
  • List of supported Composable Storefront releases; includes supported backends 
  • Angular and Node.js dependencies matrix

 

3 – Review Validity and Compatibility of all Features, Services and Updates  

 
The ecosystem surrounding your SAP Commerce Cloud installation includes many more components. You must maintain these components to stay compatible with the SAP Commerce Cloud releases. SAP has made significant efforts to align the releases of components, such as Composable Storefront.

Solr

An important component of Commerce Cloud is Solr and requires independent lifecycle management. While SAP Commerce Cloud follows a continuous innovation release model in which monthly updates are current for a minimum of 6 months from their release date, Solr updates are not fully aligned with Commerce Cloud update releases but follow its own version validity and maintenance lifecycle. As a result, Solr upgrades require proactive monitoring as well to remain supported. Be sure to plan your activities according to the Solr Version Validity Policy and update your installation of Solr before the expiration date. Current supported versions of Solr are v9.8 and v9.10. Customers running v9.8 must update to 9.10 before July 2026.  
 
For more information, refer to the following documentation:
  • Minor Solr Version Validity
  • Upgrading to Solr 9.x
SAP offers the Search Service, a fully managed SaaS solution that removes the need for customer‑driven Solr upgrades while providing the same core search capabilities and enabling AI‑driven enhancements through Intelligent Selling Services.

Migration of Cloud Portal to Kyma Runtime

Cloud Portal is migrating from BTP Neo to Kyma Runtime in March. This introduces breaking changes for API and CLI users, who must transition to Technical User authentication. Old authentication methods will stop working after migration. A test landscape is available to test the Technical User Management feature before the migration. For more information, see Migration of Cloud Portal to Kyma Runtime.

Ingress Web Server Upgrade

To implement the latest security patches and fixes, SAP will upgrade Ingress web server. For more information, see Upgrade of Ingress Web Server.

Deletion of Deprecated Password Hashing Algorithms

The deprecated password hashing algorithms are deleted. See More

Deletion of Deprecated Accelerator UIs and OCC Template Extensions

In alignment with the SAP Commerce Cloud mission to provide a modern, headless commerce experience with easier upgrades of back-end functionality, the deprecated Accelerator UIs are planned to be removed in September 2027, and the end of the extended adoption period is September 2028. See More
 
Bookmark and review the presentation Going Headless.

Deprecated AddOn Based OCC V2 Extensions

In alignment with the SAP Commerce Cloud mission to provide a modern, headless commerce experience with easier upgrades of back-end functionality, deprecated AddOn-based OCC v2 extensions will be removed and the end of mainstream maintenance ends in Q3 2027. See More

What’s New?

Details about what’s available now and what you can expect in upcoming releases can be found here:
 
For Commerce Cloud 2211:
  • About SAP Commerce Cloud Release 2211
  • What’s New in SAP Commerce Cloud
  • SAP Commerce Cloud Product Road Map
  • Notification of Key Technical Updates
For Composable Storefront:
  • About Composable Storefront
  • What’s New in Composable Storefront
  • SAP Commerce Cloud Product Road Map

entry.title: 𝗔𝗜 and 𝗦𝗔𝗣, we should think more about it in another way
entry.content:
𝗔𝗜 and 𝗦𝗔𝗣: “How does AI fits to SAP?”
It’s becoming a real architectural conversation

-> What is AI ?
AI is not just automation. It’s about AI agents that can:
- Understand context
- Make decisions
- Take actions across systems
- Continuously learn and improve
=> This means digital co-workers inside your team and landscape

-> Where Does It Fit in SAP Architecture?
- Not replacing SAP
- AI sits as an intelligent layer on top of it

1. Core ERP -> The foundation
- SAP S/4HANA
- Finance, Supply Chain, Procurement, Sales
=> SAP continues to be the single source of truth

2. Business Technology Platform (BTP) -> The Enabler
- Integration Suite -> connects SAP & non-SAP systems
- Extension Suite -> build custom AI-driven apps
- AI Core & AI Foundation -> deploy models
=> SAP BTP will be the playground for AI agents

3. AI Layer -> The Brain
- LLM-powered agents (planning)
- Task-specific agents (Finance agent, Supply Chain agents, and more)
- Multi-agent orchestration
=> These agents don’t just analyze they are acting

4. Data Layer -> The Benzin
- SAP HANA / Datasphere
- Databricks, APIs, third-party systems
=> High-quality data = smarter decisions

5. Execution Layer -> Here the Value happens, agents trigger actions via:
- APIs
- SAP Fiori Apps
- Workflows
=> A Supply Chain agent detects delay → re-plans inventory → triggers purchase order →notifies stakeholders

6. Closed-loop intelligent automation (End-to-End Flow)
- Business event occurs in SAP 
- Agent picks up the signal via BTP
- AI evaluates options using historical (real-time data)
- Decision is made (maybe recommended)
- Action is executed in SAP
- Feedback loop improves future decisions

One example in theorie:
The Finance Agent = Auto-reconciles accounts, detects anomalies, suggests journal entries
The Procurement Agent = Chooses optimal vendor based on price, delivery, risk
The Supply Chain Agent = Predicts disruptions and autonomously re-plans supply
The Sales Agent = Recommends pricing strategies and next-best actions

7. Take care about possible challenges
- Data quality and governance model
- Security and compliance model
- Explainability of AI decisions
- Complexity and Integration
- Needed change management
=> AI is powerful and need an opion for controlling
=> AI will not replace anyone a human being
=> AI will unlock the next level of SAP ERP speed and intelligence
=> The future of SAP landscapes will not only be transactional, it will be:
- Self-optimizing
- Predictive
- Autonom

entry.title: Re: Introducing UI5 Plugins for Claude: AI-Powered Development for SAPUI5 and OpenUI5
entry.content:
Hi Community,

"

Rather than relying on the LLM's general training data, which can be outdated or incomplete for framework details, these plugins embed current, maintained guidelines directly into the development workflow. The result is that Claude Code provides UI5-accurate suggestions from Day One, so you don't have to paste documentation into every conversation.

Last year, we published the UI5 MCP server described in Give Your AI Agent Some Tools: Introducing the UI5 MCP Server. The introduction of agent skills over the last year makes some tasks easier to complete compared to using only the UI5 MCP server. MCP servers aren't going away; this just introduces a new way to distribute tasks between MCP servers or skills. The choice depends on the task: Skills work well for tasks described in free text, while MCP servers offer better support for tasks that require precise input and output.

"

 

Thanks for your selfless sharing again, it really help us a lot.

🙂

 

Regards,

ZFiori.

entry.title: NSE und DataAging Felder
entry.content:
Hello,
Is it possible to populate the DataAging fields of tables in the same way as with “dead” DataAging, and then use this field to perform partitioning via NSE without the drawbacks of DataAging?

We do not use DataAging, but would like to use NSE to evict data from RAM. However, most tables do not provide a suitable criterion.

Example: Workflows
SWP_JOIN
SWP_NODEWI
SWPNODE
SWPNODELOG
SWPSTEPLOG
SWWCNTP0
SWW_CONT
SWW_CONTOB
SWWLOGHIST
SWWFLEXPROC
SWWWICOMMENT
SWF_FLEX_COLL_AT

Only SWWLOGHIST has the METH_EDATE field as a suitable criterion.

We would like to partition all of the above tables using the DataAging fields and overwrite certain partitions via NSE. To do this, we would like to propagate the contents of SWWLOGHIST-METH_DATE (if necessary, using a custom program) to the DataAging fields of all involved tables (daily run).

Will we encounter any issues with this approach, or are the DataAging fields freely available as long as the DataAging business function DAAG_DATA_AGING is inactive?
Translated with DeepL.com (free version)

****************************************************************************
Hallo,

kann man die DataAging-Felder von Tabellen analog zum toten DataAging füllen, dann die Partitionierung per NSE über dieses Feld vornehmen, ohne die Nachteile von DataAging zu haben. 

Wir haben DataAging nicht im Einsatz, würden aber gerne Daten per NSE aus dem RAM verdrängen. Die meisten Tabellen stellen aber kein geeignetes Kriterium bereit.

Beispiel: Workflows
SWP_JOIN 
SWP_NODEWI  
SWPNODE 
SWPNODELOG  
SWPSTEPLOG  
SWWCNTP0  
SWW_CONT  
SWW_CONTOB  
SWWLOGHIST     
SWWFLEXPROC  
SWWWICOMMENT  
SWF_FLEX_COLL_AT  

Nur SWWLOGHIST hat als adäquates Kriterium das Feld METH_EDATE.

Wir würden gerne alle obigen Tabellen über die DataAging-Felder partitionieren und bestimme Partitionen per NSE verdrängen.  Hierzu würden wir gerne den Inhalt von SWWLOGHIST-METH_DATE (zur Not auch mit einem eigenen Programm) in die DataAgingfelder aller beteiligen Tabellen vererben (täglicher Lauf). 

Bekommen wir bei dieser Vorgehensweise ein Problem oder stehen die DataAging-Felder frei zu Verfügung, solange die Businessfunction DAAG_DATA_AGING des DataAging inaktiv ist?

entry.title: Re: Account assignment type field blank in journal entry...
entry.content:
The Account Assignment Type (ACDOCA-ACCASTY) is only filled for postings to primary or secondary cost elements and remains empty for balance sheet accounts, even if a WBS element or cost center is entered.

Please refer to below KBA for more details:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003736086

entry.title: Approval workflow for Vendor, Customer and Material Master in SAP Public Cloud 2602
entry.content:
Requirement:
We request guidance and feasibility confirmation for implementing an automated Customer, Vendor and Material Master upload process with a Maker–Checker authorization mechanism in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Business Scenario:
Customer, Vendor and Material Master data should be uploaded automatically (e.g., via file upload or interface). The uploaded data must go through a Maker–Checker (Create and Approve) workflow, where:

  • The Maker uploads or creates the master data

  • The Checker reviews and approves the data

  • Only approved data is posted to the system

Support Required:
Please advise on:

  1. Whether this scenario is supported in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

  2. Relevant standard functionality, Fiori apps, or SSCUI configurations

  3. Use of SAP workflow, APIs, or extensibility options to enable this requirement

  4. Known limitations or recommended best practices

Kindly provide guidance on feasibility and next steps.

 

Regards,
Tushar Sonawane

entry.title: AI Developer Challenge April 2026 - build AI Agents with Generative AI Hub
entry.content:
For this month's coding challenge on the SAP Community, you will have the opportunity to learn more about the Orchestration Workflow available on Generative AI Hub with SAP AI Core. You will also work on building your first code-based agents with CrewAI and LiteLLM.
What you'll learn: Build AI solutions that combine Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with intelligent agents. You will learn how to work with the APIs of the SAP Cloud SDK for AI to create an orchestration workflow including document grounding, and build code-based agents that leverage grounded document information to provide accurate, context-aware responses.
 
The coding challenge will span over the next four weeks where you will be working on 4 separate challenges, one for each week. At the end of each week you need to upload the results of the given week's challenge. This can be a screenshot or a configuration file, but no worries, each week's challenge will exactly tell you what to do.
 
But first, let's set the stage.

Setting the stage

The United Nations has designated 2026 as the International Year of Volunteers and we want you to think about what such an initiative could be.
 
Why this matters: Volunteer organizations often struggle with information accessibility - experienced knowledge is scattered across documents, reports, and guides. By combining AI orchestration with document grounding, you can create intelligent assistants that make this knowledge instantly accessible to volunteers in the field, enabling them to make better decisions and have greater impact.
 
For this month's coding challenge, you'll build a system that could support volunteer initiatives by grounding and orchestrating contextual information to a Large Language Model (LLM). Here is the use case for this challenge:

Social Services in Germany

For this month's code challenge, you will build an intelligent solution for social welfare service recipients to fetch information about the "Grundsicherung", a social service for unemployed citizens in Germany. On top of that the service should help social welfare service recipients to find information about food banks in Germany, these food banks are provided through an organization called "Tafel Deutschland".

The Challenge

Now that you have a better understanding of the technology stack used and the architecture, let's talk about the challenge.
 
Over four weeks, you'll progress from basic document grounding to building intelligent agents that can reason with domain-specific knowledge, all using Jupyter Notebooks with pre-configured infrastructure.

Prerequisites

To successfully complete this challenge, you should have:
  • Basic Python programming experience
  • Familiarity with Jupyter Notebooks
  • Understanding of REST APIs (helpful but not required)
  • Interest in AI/ML concepts (no deep expertise needed)

Environment Setup

You will work through the challenge using Jupyter Notebooks in Business Application Studio. Here you can comfortably use a Python environment and a pre-defined environment to work on the challenges.
You don't have to take care of the infrastructure setup. We will provide you with access to a fully functioning system to work through the Jupyter Notebooks.

Weekly Structure

The challenge is structured as follows:

Week 1: Grounding with SAP Generative AI Hub

What you'll learn:
  • Connect to the Orchestration Service: Access SAP's Generative AI Hub orchestration service, which provides a unified interface to multiple LLM models through a single deployment
  • Configure Document Grounding: Set up the document grounding service to retrieve relevant context from a vector database before generating responses
  • Implement RAG Pattern: Build a complete RAG workflow that combines document retrieval with LLM generation to provide contextually accurate answers
  • Use Prompt Templates: Create structured prompts that incorporate both user queries and grounded context from your knowledge base

Week 2: Building AI Agents with CrewAI and LiteLLM

What you'll learn:
  • Build Your First AI Agent: Create an intelligent agent with a specific role, goal, and backstory using the CrewAI framework
  • Define Agent Tasks: Structure concrete objectives and expected outputs that guide agent behavior
  • Integrate Multiple Technologies: Connect CrewAI with SAP Generative AI Hub models through LiteLLM's unified API
  • Process Complex Queries: Enable agents to analyze user input, make decisions, and provide structured recommendations

Week 3: AI Agents with Grounding Tools

What you'll learn:
  • Create Custom Agent Tools: Build a custom tool that wraps the document grounding service, making it accessible to your AI agent
  • Enable Tool-Based Reasoning: Allow agents to decide when and how to use tools based on user queries
  • Integrate RAG with Agents: Combine retrieval-augmented generation with agentic workflows for more accurate, knowledge-grounded responses

Week 4: Predict Missing Data Points using SAP-RPT-1

What you'll learn:

  • Predicts missing data points  with SAP-RPT-1 (SAP's Foundation model): As it is a pre-trained model, it can do regression and classification tasks without the need to train your own model.
  • More instructions and data to follow 🙂

Submission Requirements

At the end of each week, you'll need to submit evidence of your completed challenge as a comment under this blog post. Just post a screenshot or agents your output under THIS POST.

How you will get access

Click this link to generate a user and access your workbook in our trial system: TBD

Additional Information

The Solution Diagram

For this challenge you are using different systems to achieve an automated grounding process and you will set up an orchestration workflow on Generative AI Hub.

 

SAP BTP Architecture for Grounding and Orchestration Workflow

This architecture diagram illustrates the complete system setup for implementing a RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) solution using SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform) services integrated with partner foundation models.
The data, in this case, is stored within an S3 Bucket on AWS (Amazon Web Services) managed through the Document Store service on BTP. As vector database, this solution uses the SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine.

Main Components:

SAP BTP Platform
The entire solution runs on SAP Business Technology Platform:
  1. Generative AI Hub - The AI service layer containing
    • SAP AI Launchpad: User interface for managing AI scenarios and deployments
    • SAP AI Core: The core AI orchestration engine containing:
    • Orchestration Workflow: Coordinates the entire RAG process by querying the LLM and triggering grounding
    • Grounding Service: Handles document fetching, chunking, and vectorization
    • Foundation Model Access: Provides connectivity to external LLMs via HTTPS
  2. SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry runtime - Contains:
    • S3 Bucket (AWS) Document Store: Cloud storage for grounding documents that the AI Engineer uploads

External Components:

Partner Foundation Models
  • Foundation Model Providers: Provides LLM and embedding models via HTTPS connection (e.g., Gemini, Azure OpenAI, Amazon Bedrock)
  • Extensible architecture supporting multiple model providers
AI Engineer with Bruno
  • The human operator who uploads documents to the S3 bucket using Bruno (API client tool)
  • The human operator who manages secrets in SAP AI Core using Bruno

Data Flow:

  1. Upload Flow (Purple arrow): AI Engineer uploads documents via Bruno to the S3 bucket
  2. Vectorization Flow (Orange arrow): Grounding Service processes documents and stores embeddings in HANA Cloud Vector Engine
  3. Grounding Flow (Blue arrow): Fetches grounding documents from storage
  4. Similarity Search Flow (Green arrow): Orchestration Workflow queries HANA Cloud's Vector Engine for relevant context

SAP HANA Cloud (Vector Engine)

The vector database that stores document embeddings and performs cosine similarity searches to retrieve relevant context for user queries.
This architecture enables a complete RAG implementation where documents are grounded (vectorized and stored), and user queries are enriched with relevant context before being sent to foundation models for response generation.

Understand what is happening

To fully understand the single steps that need to be taken, you can use the following sequence diagram.

 

Grounding Document & Vectorization Process on SAP BTP

This sequence diagram illustrates the complete workflow for setting up a document grounding pipeline and orchestration workflow using SAP AI Core on SAP BTP (running on AWS infrastructure).
 

1: Credentials & Upload

The process begins with the user creating an instance of the SAP BTP Object Store service in Cloud Foundry, which provisions an S3 bucket on AWS. The user receives service credentials and uses Bruno (an API client) to upload grounding documents directly to the S3 bucket. These documents will serve as the knowledge base for the RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) system.

2: Pipeline Setup

Next, the user configures the infrastructure through SAP AI Launchpad by creating a Resource Group. Using Bruno or SAP AI Launchpad, they create a generic secret in SAP AI Core (storing credentials for external services like partner foundation model providers) and then create a grounding pipeline. AI Core returns a pipeline ID that will be used to execute the grounding process.

3: Grounding & Vectorization

When triggered, SAP AI Core activates the Grounding Service, which fetches the documents from S3. The service chunks the documents into smaller segments and preprocesses them for embedding. These chunks are then sent to an Embedding Model (from partner providers like Gemini, Azure OpenAI, or Amazon Bedrock), which converts them into vector representations. The resulting embeddings are stored in SAP HANA Cloud's Vector Engine, creating a searchable vector database.

4: Orchestration & Querying

Finally, the user creates an orchestration workflow in SAP AI Launchpad and configures it to use the grounding pipeline. When a query is submitted, the Orchestrator performs a cosine similarity search in HANA Cloud to retrieve the most relevant document chunks. These chunks are then passed as context to a Foundation Model (LLM), which generates a contextual response. The orchestrated answer is returned to the user, combining the grounded knowledge with the LLM's generative capabilities.
 
This architecture demonstrates a complete RAG implementation using SAP's AI services, enabling volunteers or coordinators to query domain-specific knowledge with accurate, contextually grounded responses.

entry.title: Re: Is it possible to combine a filter with the exportPDF...
entry.content:
Hi @Fiona_ ,

You can pass the Input Control Selection to the ExportToPDF scripting object , you can use the script on a button to control the output of the export.

var sel = InputControl_1.getInputControlDataSource().getActiveSelectedMembers();

ExportToPDF_1.setBatchExportEnabled(true);
ExportToPDF_1.getBatchExportDataSource("Enter Model Name").setDimensionFilter("CompanyCode",sel);
ExportToPDF_1.exportView();

 Regards

Shivam

entry.title: Early Adopter Care - Central, Unified View of HR Documents in Latest People Profile for all Roles
entry.content:
As of today, viewing and accessing  documents in SuccessFactors isn't unified across the roles. IT admins have a comprehensive view through Manage Documents, but the permission model is basic and not designed for everyday business users. Meanwhile, employees, managers, and HR professionals must navigate multiple screens and modules to track down a single attachment or customers need to rely on partner solutions that provide a unified view of all documents stored for a user.

The feedback we've heard is clear: customers want a lightweight, central, unified place to go to see documents inside SuccessFactors—one that's secure, compliant, and easy for all stakeholders to use.

We’re now happy to launch an Early Adopter Care program to pilot such a central, role-based attachments experience natively inside Latest People Profile. 

Our Vision: One Profile, All Documents, Every Role

With this pilot, Latest People Profile becomes the single access point for viewing HR documents related to employees and new hires—across all user roles.

The new experience will provide role-based access that respects existing system permissions and GDPR compliance, unified visibility for employees, managers, HR, and admins, and business-independent document actions, such as view and download. 

The new feature isn't about replacing advanced document management platforms—it's about giving users a simple, central place to start when they need to find or share HR documents.

What Early Adopter Care Participants Will Get And Who Should Join

By joining the pilot, you'll be among the first to experience the unified document view inside Latest People Profile. You'll work closely with our product management and development teams to shape the feature with your expertise, and benefit from ongoing guidance during the pilot, and prioritized bug fixes.

This program is ideal for organizations that want a simple, centrally accessible document view in SuccessFactors without the overhead of a full document management solution. We're looking for companies open to piloting with a small, cross-functional user group—ideally including HR, managers, employees, and IT—to validate access rules and user experience.

How to Participate

If you are interested in this time-boxed engagement as an early adopter care customer, please see the registration page with prerequisites for joining the EAC program here: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/campaign/4197 
On this page you will also find the link to register for the EAC program.

Looking forward to your registration 👍

Best Regards, 

Sylvia Strangfeld,

Product Management - SAP SuccessFactors Technology Platform

entry.title: Re: Incorrect GI Date in schedule line1 post plant substi...
entry.content: Yes, this is standard SAP behavior. When the plant is changed during re-ATP (VA02) or BOP, the system does not always recalculate the GI date for the first schedule line according to the new route. Instead, it retains the old route’s dates, while subsequent schedule lines (like line 2) are recalculated correctly.

entry.title: Re: Important Update: Review Onboarding User Permissions
entry.content:
Hello @smitajalit,

We are in process of configuring Latest Full Profile. Based on this article it is recommended to remove below permissions from the end-user roles. 

  • Employee Central HRIS OData API (read-only)
  • Employee Central HRIS OData API (editable)

We followed this instruction especially due to below negative impacts.

  • When migrating to Latest People Profile – Full Profile, more fields will be visible to the user than expected, which can lead to data exposure risks.
  • These permissions were previously needed for Onboarding but are now only required for technical users managing integrations.

However, after removal of these permissions from end-user roles, now Hiring Manager cannot create a Job Requisition because of error "User has no API Visibility on object location", please see attached.

I followed SAP KBA 2607545 - Error "User has no API Visibility on object" when Creating Job Requisition from Position - Recruiting Management and Employee Central but in resolution it is specified to grant permission to Employee Central Foundation OData API (editable) which is contradictory with this article.

It is not acceptable that more fields will be visible to the user than expected, which can lead to data exposure risks. Thus, I'm not able to grant permission to Employee Central Foundation OData API (editable) for end-user roles.

Can you please provide solution that will fix this error?

Thanks,
